title: ABC Classification
text: abc() requires grouped tibble lazy DBI object using dplyr::group_by() specify group composition drives contribution Value Capture .value provided, column aggregated per group member; otherwise, counts rows Category Values Provide break points used set cumulative categories break point get letter category starting ‘’ want see stores make top 40% revenue follow top 70% 90% put c(0.4,.7,.9,1) Table 1 execute abc(), console return segment_abc object displaying custom print message: summary function’s actions Details category break points labels Describes main transformation steps columns referenced Lists possible next actions Use calculate() generate ABC classification table lazy DBI object. Use dplyr::collect() return tibble. Table 2 table contains grouped data various metrics, highlighting contribution group terms value transaction count. explanation key columns interpret results:
code: # Example  contoso::sales |>     dplyr::group_by(store_key) |>     fpa::abc(       category_values = c(0.4,.7,.9,1),        .value = margin       )  ── ABC Classification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Function: `ABC` was executed  ── Description: ──  This calculates a rolling cumulative distribution of variable and segments each group member's contribution by the break points provided. Helpful to know which group member's proportational contribution to the total.  ── Category Information ──  • The data set is summarized by store_key and then sums each group member's margin contribution of the total margin and then finally calculates each groups rolling cumulative porportion of the total • Then cumulative distribution was then arranged from lowest to highest and finally classified into 4 break points 40%, 70%, 90%, 100%  and labelled into the following categories a, b, c, d ── Actions: ── ✔Aggregate ✖Shift ✖Compare ✔Proportion Of Total ✖Count Distinct  ── Next Steps: ──  • Use `calculate()` to return the results ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── contoso::sales |>     dplyr::group_by(store_key) |>     fpa::abc(       category_values = c(0.4,.7,.9,1),        .value = margin       ) |>     fpa::calculate()

title: Standard vs. Non-Standard Calendars
text: many Financial Planning & Analysis (FP&) functions, ’ll encounter calendar_type argument. might confusing, people assume one type calendar! accommodate unique operational analytical needs certain industries (particularly retail manufacturing), FP&often involves two primary calendar systems: Standard Calendar (Gregorian) Non-Standard Calendars (e.g., 5-4-4, 4-4-5, 4-5-4) guide explains differences calendars typical use cases business analysis. conventional calendar used general financial reporting, scheduling, external communication. Period Structure: 12 months varying lengths (28–31 days). Days/Weeks: 365 days per year (366 leap year). Advantage: matches external reporting deadlines, regulatory timelines, standard corporate schedules. primary drawback comparing operational metrics (like sales margin) month--month (MoM) quarter--quarter (QoQ) challenging. varying number days weekends leads misleading conclusions. example, comparing sales 31-day month 28-day month inherently show “drop” total revenue, even daily performance identical. makes true performance comparison difficult. Non-standard calendars explicitly designed control period--period variance. achieve ensuring every comparable period identical number weeks , critically, identical number weekends. allows cleaner “apples--apples” comparison operational performance. common types Retail Calendars (used retail, restaurant, manufacturing), 5-4-4, 4-4-5, 4-5-4. 5-4-4 structure ensures every quarter exactly 13 weeks long, month structure repeats perfectly: Basis: Weeks period, days month. Total Year: 52 weeks (364 days). Since 52-week year 364 days, solar year ≈365.25\approx 365.25 days, 53rd week must added every 5 6 years keep fiscal year aligned actual calendar year. 53-week year crucial planning consideration businesses using non-standard calendars. calendar_type argument instruct function system use internal calculations indexing. use traditional calendar, pass: "standard" use retail calendar, pass respective structure: "544", "454", "445" hood, fpaR handles complexities: generates appropriate date keys ensures metrics indexed correctly start end chosen calendar’s periods.

what: Why do we need this package when we have lubridate?
title: Getting started
text: Lubridate excellent package core many fpa’s functions. issue isn’t lubridate rather challenges issues dataset typically don’t allow directly use lubridate. advantage package perform required pre-processing steps . Issue 1: Many datasets continuous dates, especially data recorded business days active transactions Issue 2: Period imbalances periods (Eg. different number days February vs. January) can create misleading analysis/trends analysis requires non-standard calendar types Issue 3: Calculating time intelligence groups can lead larger memory issues even smaller datasets Referencing Table 1 , use dplyr::lag() compare Day--Day (DoD) margin, missing 2024-01-02, 2024-01-04, 2024-01-05 lead incorrect answers trends. Table 1: Incomplete calendar table can lead wrong conclusions trends correct , fpa automatically complete calendar group missing periods ensure missing periods calculating trends. Table 2 see complete calendar set new column, “missing_date_indicator” indicate many dates missing. Table 2: Original table now complete missing dates missing date indicator comparing two performance periods standard calendar, can often compare periods unequal number days periods. example want compare January sales February can get misleading conclusions due unequal number weekends days periods. practice two choices: compare periods similar days (eg. 28th February compares compare 28th January) omit three days January sales together compare imbalanced comparison (eg. 28th February compares 31st January days lost). package second option ensure don’t loose January’s sales help flag imbalance, fpa add column let know many periods (eg. days) comparison period increase transparency dynamic. create example, use pmtd() function calculate prior month date cumulative margin current month. pass ti object calculate() filter results February 2022, see Table 3. 2022-02-28, see comparing 31 days previous period 28 days current period. Table 3 data isn’t already database fpa leverage duckdb convert data enable larger memory calculation. necessary time intelligence functions grouped data, need complete calendar group combination. even modest datasets, can quickly multiple grow data larger memory. data already database, fpa use dbplyr convert functions SQL write queries. either scenario, can use dplyr::collect() execute SQL query return tibble local computer.
code: contoso::sales |>    pmtd(order_date,margin,"standard",lag_n = 1) # quickly load contoso package to a database via the contoso package db <- contoso::create_contoso_duckdb()  # pass through to the same function mtd() db$sales |> mtd(order_date,margin,"standard")  # same syntax even though data source is now a tibble  contoso::sales |> mtd(order_date,margin,"standard")

title: Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a DBI lazy frame or tibble object — augment_calendar
text: function takes data frame date column generates wide set derived date attributes. include start/end dates year, quarter, month, week; day--week indicators; completed remaining days time unit; additional convenience variables weekend indicators. designed time-based feature engineering useful reporting, forecasting, time-series modeling, data enrichment workflows.

title: Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a DBI lazy frame or tibble object — augment_calendar
text: dbi  tibble containing original data along generated date-based attributes.

title: Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a DBI lazy frame or tibble object — augment_calendar
text: function creates following groups attributes: 1. Start end dates year_start_date, year_end_date quarter_start_date, quarter_end_date month_start_date, month_end_date week_start_date, week_end_date 2. Day--week fields day_of_week – numeric day week (1–7) day_of_week_label – ordered factor label (e.g., Mon, Tue, …) 3. Duration fields days_in_year – total days year interval days_in_quarter – total days quarter interval days_in_month – total days month 4. Completed/remaining days days_complete_in_week, days_remaining_in_week days_complete_in_month, days_remaining_in_month days_complete_in_quarter, days_remaining_in_quarter days_complete_in_year, days_remaining_in_year 5. Miscellaneous weekend_indicator – equals 1 Saturday Sunday; otherwise 0 date-derived fields ending _date coerced class Date.

title: Generate a Cross-Dialect SQL Date Series — seq_date_sql
text: Creates lazy dbplyr table containing continuous sequence dates. function automatically detects SQL dialect connection dispatches efficient native series generator (e.g., GENERATE_SERIES DuckDB/Postgres GENERATOR Snowflake).

title: Generate a Cross-Dialect SQL Date Series — seq_date_sql
code: seq_date_sql(   .con,   start_date,   end_date,   calendar_type = "standard",   time_unit = "day",   week_start = 7 )

title: Generate a Cross-Dialect SQL Date Series — seq_date_sql
text: .con valid DBI connection object (e.g., DuckDB, Postgres, Snowflake) dbplyr simulated connection. start_date character string 'YYYY-MM-DD' format Date object representing start series. end_date character string 'YYYY-MM-DD' format Date object representing end series. time_unit character string specifying interval. Must one : "day", "week", "month", "quarter", "year". week_start description

title: Generate a Cross-Dialect SQL Date Series — seq_date_sql
text: tbl_lazy (SQL) object single column date.

title: Generate a Cross-Dialect SQL Date Series — seq_date_sql
text: function designed "nestable," meaning resulting SQL can used safely inside larger dplyr pipelines. avoids clauses dialects like DuckDB prevent parser errors dbplyr wraps query subquery (e.g., SELECT * (...) q01). unit testing, function supports dbplyr simulation objects. TestConnection detected, returns lazy_frame avoid metadata field queries otherwise fail mock connection.
